claim_text: Evaluation.For NARCBENCH-CORE, we evaluate via leave-domain-out cross-validation: for each of the 17 domains, we train the deception direction and any run-level classifiers on the remaining 16 domains and evaluate on the held-out domain.
evidence_text: Core results are the mean across folds. For TRANSFER and STEGO, we train on all of COREand evaluate zero-shot.

claim_text: We introduce NARCBENCH, a benchmark for evaluating collusion detection under environment distribution shift, and propose five probing techniques that aggregate per-agent deception scores to classify scenarios at the group level.
evidence_text: Our probes achieve 1.00 AUROC in-distribution and 0.60–0.86 AUROC when transferred zero-shot to structurally different multi-agent scenarios and a steganographic blackjack card-counting task. We find that no single probing technique dominates across all collusion types, suggesting that different forms of collusion manifest differently in activation space.

claim_text: We introduce five probing techniques that operate on the set of deception scores (or activations) across all agents in a scenario, spanning the three distributed detection paradigms of Section 2.
evidence_text: Sorted Concatenation implementscentralised fusion, ingesting full activation vectors from all agents. Peak Suspicion, Deception Split, and Asymmetry Probe implementdecision-level fusion, each combining per-agent scalar deception scores through a different aggregation rule.

claim_text: In this work, we show that such collusion can be detected by inspecting agents’ internal activations, even when their text outputs appear entirely normal.
evidence_text: Proposed defences against covert coordination include monitoring text outputs, paraphrasing agent communications, and imposing behavioural constraints [Greenblatt et al., 2024, Syrnikov et al., 2026]. However, these have been shown to fail against steganographic signals designed to be indistinguishable from natural language [Motwani et al., 2024, Mathew et al., 2025].

claim_text: Our approach proceeds in two stages: learning a deception direction from individual agents, then aggregating scores across the group to detect collusion.
